scorecards.title: Recycled Water & Conservation
scorecards.description: Since 1988, Cerritos has operated one of the region's most successful recycled water programs, replacing potable water with treated recycled water for irrigation across parks, medians, and school grounds.
scorecards.tldr: Cerritos saves 722 million gallons of drinking water every year by using recycled water to irrigate parks and public spaces.
highlights: 722 million gallons of potable water conserved every year
25 miles of recycled water distribution lines serve 200+ acres
Parks, medians, school grounds, and golf courses all use recycled water
Program has operated continuously since 1988 — nearly 4 decades
challenges: Expanding recycled water to additional commercial and residential uses
Aging infrastructure requires ongoing maintenance investment
Climate change increasing demand for drought-resistant irrigation

source.documentName: City of Cerritos — Recycled Water Program

scorecards.title: Urban Forestry & Tree City USA
scorecards.description: Cerritos maintains over 28,000 public trees across streets, parks, and medians. The city has been recognized as a Tree City USA by the National Arbor Day Foundation for 25+ consecutive years, planting approximately 250 new trees annually.
scorecards.tldr: With 28,000+ trees and 25+ years as Tree City USA, Cerritos is serious about its urban canopy for cleaner air and cooler streets.
highlights: 28,000+ public trees — one of the greenest cities in the LA metro
Tree City USA for 25+ consecutive years from the Arbor Day Foundation
~250 new trees planted each year to replace damaged or aging trees
$4.5M invested in parks and trees management (FY 2025-26)
challenges: Drought stress and climate impacts increasing tree mortality rates
Need more drought-tolerant species in future plantings
Balancing tree canopy growth with urban development pressures

source.documentName: City of Cerritos — Current Green Efforts

scorecards.title: Solar Energy & Clean Power
scorecards.description: Cerritos was a solar energy pioneer — City Hall featured one of the nation's first solar heating systems when it opened in 1978. Today, the Corporate Yard solar array generates 350,000 kWh/year, LED traffic signals save 60% energy, and the city participates in the Magnolia Power Plant clean energy partnership.
scorecards.tldr: From the nation's first solar City Hall in 1978 to today's 350,000 kWh solar farm, Cerritos has been a clean energy leader for decades.
highlights: City Hall solar heating system installed 1978 — one of the nation's first
Corporate Yard solar array generates 350,000 kWh per year
LED traffic signals across the city save 60% energy vs. incandescent
Magnolia Power Plant partnership provides clean energy for city facilities
challenges: Expanding solar to additional city facilities and parking structures
Battery storage needed to maximize solar generation value
Aging City Hall system needs modernization for current technology

source.documentName: City of Cerritos — Current Green Efforts

scorecards.title: Clean Vehicle Fleet & CNG
scorecards.description: Cerritos operates a Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) fueling station built in 2007. All Athens Services waste collection vehicles are CNG-powered. The Cerritos on Wheels (COW) transit buses run on propane, reducing emissions from city transportation.
scorecards.tldr: Every trash truck and city bus in Cerritos runs on cleaner fuel — CNG for waste vehicles and propane for transit buses.
highlights: 100% of Athens Services waste hauling vehicles are CNG-operated
City-owned CNG fueling station operational since 2007
Cerritos on Wheels (COW) transit fleet runs on propane
Reducing diesel emissions across all city fleet operations
challenges: Transitioning to electric vehicles as EV technology matures
CNG infrastructure maintenance and upgrade costs
Evaluating electric bus options for the COW transit service

source.documentName: City of Cerritos — Current Green Efforts

scorecards.title: Fiscal Health & Transparency
scorecards.description: Cerritos maintains strong fiscal health with a General Fund reserve policy, GFOA Distinguished Budget Presentation Award, and public financial transparency through the OpenGov portal. FY 2025-26 includes a strategic $2M CalPERS Additional Discretionary Payment (ADP) to reduce long-term pension liability.
scorecards.tldr: Cerritos is financially healthy with award-winning budget transparency and smart moves to pay down pension debt early.
highlights: GFOA Distinguished Budget Presentation Award recipient
$101.5M General Fund revenue with diversified income sources
$2M CalPERS ADP payment to reduce long-term pension liability
OpenGov transparency portal provides public access to 10 years of data
challenges: Sales tax headwinds from auto sales slowdown ($46M forecast adjusted to $42.8M)
Rising public safety contract costs (Sheriff + Fire)
CalPERS unfunded liability remains a statewide challenge

source.documentName: City of Cerritos Combined Financial Program FY 2025-2026
